Transfer Report Abstract - Liquid-Crystal Photonics

Candidate: A. Researcher
Programme: DPhil in Engineering Science

This report describes progress towards voxel-resolved control of the
optical axis in liquid-crystal polymer networks written by two-photon
polymerization. Conventional geometric-phase optics rely on a planar
photoalignment layer that fixes the director pattern across the whole
film thickness; the present work instead exploits the confinement of the
two-photon voxel to imprint depth-varying director profiles, opening a
route to genuinely three-dimensional flat optics.

During the first year, a Nanoscribe-based writing protocol was developed
for a commercial reactive mesogen mixture. The protocol yields optically
uniform structures over 200 x 200 micrometre fields with birefringence
retained after development. Polarisation gratings fabricated with this
protocol show first-order diffraction efficiencies competitive with
photoaligned references, while additionally supporting twist gradients
along the optical path that a single alignment surface cannot produce.

Remaining challenges include polymerization-induced shrinkage, which
distorts tall structures, and the limited thermal stability of the
unpolymerized mesogen film during long writes. Both are addressed in the
work plan for the second year, alongside the headline goal: a stacked,
achromatic Pancharatnam-Berry lens operating across the visible band.
